title: SEGMENT 4 — Library prep: fragmenting, adapter ligation, indexing

lines.text: SEGMENT 4 — Library prep: fragmenting, adapter ligation, indexing

Before any DNA goes anywhere near a flow cell, it has to be turned into what Illumina calls a library — a collection of DNA fragments, each one of a defined size range, each one with specific synthetic sequences attached to both ends.
The library is what gets loaded onto the instrument.

Library prep is the step that converts whatever DNA you started with — genomic DNA from a blood sample, RNA from a tumor, environmental DNA from a soil sample — into a form that the Illumina chemistry can grab onto and read.
Library prep has three stages, in this order: fragmentation, end repair and adapter ligation, and indexing.
Let me walk through each.
Stage one: fragmentation.
Genomic DNA, as it comes out of a cell, is enormous — chromosomes hundreds of millions of base pairs long.
Illumina can only read short fragments, typically a few hundred base pairs at a time.
So the first thing you have to do is chop your starting DNA into pieces of roughly the right size.
There are several ways to do this.

The classical method is sonication — you put the DNA in a tube, blast it with high-frequency sound waves, and the mechanical shearing fractures the DNA into random fragments of a tunable size, depending on how long you sonicate and at what intensity.
The newer and increasingly popular method is tagmentation — using a modified enzyme called a transposase that simultaneously cuts the DNA and inserts adapter sequences in one step.
Tagmentation is faster, requires less starting material, and we'll come back to it in Segment 11 when we discuss the Nextera library prep family.
Either way, the output is the same: a tube full of DNA fragments, somewhere between two hundred and a thousand base pairs long, with random ends.
Stage two: end repair and adapter ligation.
Random fragmentation produces ragged ends — some single-stranded overhangs, some blunt, some with chemical groups in the wrong place.
The first thing you do is enzymatically "polish" the ends, making them blunt and chemically compatible with the next step.
Then you add an A-tailing step — appending a single A nucleotide to the 3-prime end of each fragment — to set up a sticky end for ligation.
Now you bring in the adapters.
The adapters are synthetic, double-stranded pieces of DNA, designed in the lab, that get ligated onto both ends of every fragment in the library.
Each adapter contains three functional regions.
First, a sequence that's complementary to the oligonucleotides anchored on the flow cell — this is what lets the fragment bind to the flow cell surface in the next stage.
Second, a primer binding site that the sequencing reaction will use to start reading.
Third, an index sequence, which we'll come to in a moment.
The adapters are the plug-and-play interface between your sample and the instrument.
Without adapters, your fragment is just a random piece of DNA.
With adapters, it has a barcode, a binding handle, and a starting line.
Stage three: indexing.
This is the trick that makes Illumina sequencing economically practical for studying many samples.
Suppose you have a hundred patient samples that you want to sequence.
You don't want to run a hundred separate flow cells — that would be expensive and slow.
Instead, during library prep, you give each sample its own unique short sequence — typically eight to ten base pairs long — that gets ligated into the adapters.
We call this the index or barcode.
Sample one gets index AAGCTT, sample two gets index TTCAGG, sample three gets index CGATAC, and so on.
After all the samples have been individually indexed, you pool them together into a single tube — multiplexing — and load the pooled library onto a single flow cell.
The instrument sequences everything together.
Then, in the data analysis step, the software reads the index portion of each read and uses it to demultiplex — that is, to sort the reads back into per-sample bins based on which index they carry.
A hundred samples on one flow cell.
A thousand samples, if you're being aggressive.
Indexing is the bookkeeping system that makes Illumina sequencing feasible at the scale of large clinical and population studies.
Here's the analogy.
Indexing is like adding return-address barcodes to a thousand different envelopes before you dump them all into the same mail sorting machine.
The machine processes them all together — much faster than processing one envelope at a time — and the barcodes let you re-sort them into the right destinations at the end.
Without indexing, you'd be stuck running one sample per flow cell.
With indexing, you can run thousands.

A subtle point that matters for any clinical work: modern Illumina libraries usually use dual indexing — two separate index sequences, one at each end of the fragment, both of which the instrument reads independently.
Dual indexing is much more robust against a failure mode called index hopping, which we'll cover in Segment 14.
For now, just know that the index sometimes lives at both ends rather than one.
There are many variants on library prep, customized for different starting materials.
RNA-seq libraries start by converting RNA to DNA with reverse transcriptase, then proceed with adapter ligation.
ChIP-seq libraries start with DNA that's been pulled down by a specific antibody.
Exome libraries undergo a hybridization-based capture step after adapter ligation, where biotinylated probes pull out only the protein-coding regions of the genome and discard everything else.
Methylation libraries use a chemical treatment called bisulfite conversion before fragmentation, to read out which cytosines are methylated.
Every one of these variants is a riff on the same theme: fragment your starting material, attach adapters, add an index, sometimes do an enrichment step, and then load it onto the flow cell.
The whole library prep workflow takes a few hours, can be automated on robots, and is now the most expensive and time-consuming bottleneck of a typical sequencing project.
The instrument run itself, ironically, is often faster and cheaper than getting the samples ready for it.
We'll come back to library prep variants in Segment 11.
For now, you have a tube of fragments, each one with adapters on both ends and a barcode in the middle of the adapter.
You're ready to load the flow cell.
